steps.title: Identify the logical signal in the passage
steps.content: Wace "introduced" the Round Table when adapting Geoffrey's History. "Introduced" means he added it — it was not in Geoffrey's original 1130s work. Since the Round Table originated with Wace in 1155, it could not have appeared in any source written before that date.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Roman de Brut is more historically accurate than History, because Culhwch and Olwen had not been written when Geoffrey of Monmouth was writing his work.': The passage is about what Wace added, not about historical accuracy or the timeline of Culhwch and Olwen. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Geoffrey of Monmouth was unaware of stories of the Round Table when composing his History, though historians know that works containing such stories were available to him.': The passage says Wace introduced the Round Table — meaning it didn't exist yet in Geoffrey's time. There is no basis to claim Geoffrey had access to Round Table stories. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Geoffrey of Monmouth's accounts of Arthurian legends in his History are more similar overall in content to the accounts in Culhwch and Olwen than they are to the accounts in Roman de Brut.': No content comparison between the works is supported by the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the Arthurian legends that the author of Vita Sancti Cadoci drew on would not have featured the Round Table.': Vita Sancti Cadoci was composed in the 11th century — before Wace introduced the Round Table in 1155. It could not have included something that didn't exist yet. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
